Article Title: A Perspective on Ovarian Cancer Biomarkers: Past, Present and Yet-To-Come
Actionable Recommendation: Utilize multivariate index assays (MIAs) such as Ova1 and Overa, which combine multiple protein biomarkers including CA125-II and HE4, for preoperative triage of ovarian tumors to determine malignancy risk and guide referral to gynecologic oncologists.

Article Title: A Perspective on Ovarian Cancer Biomarkers: Past, Present and Yet-To-Come
Actionable Recommendation: Incorporate serial ultrasound imaging combined with biomarker panels (e.g., CA125 interpreted by ROCA algorithm) to improve early detection and monitoring of ovarian tumors, enabling personalized, serial assessment of malignancy risk over time.

Article Title: A Perspective on Ovarian Cancer Biomarkers: Past, Present and Yet-To-Come
Actionable Recommendation: Develop and implement comprehensive serum biomarker panels that combine protein and nucleic acid markers (e.g., free DNA, mRNA, microRNAs, circulating tumor DNA) to enhance selectivity and accuracy in ovarian cancer diagnostics and screening.

Article Title: A Perspective on Ovarian Cancer Biomarkers: Past, Present and Yet-To-Come
Actionable Recommendation: Incorporate germ-line cancer genetic testing at birth and periodic genomic screening throughout life to identify inherited and acquired mutations, enabling personalized cancer risk assessment, behavior modification, and tailored medical surveillance.

Article Title: Recent Advances in Ovarian Cancer: Therapeutic Strategies, Potential Biomarkers, and Technological Improvements
Actionable Recommendation: Use HSP90 inhibitors such as 17-AAG to induce proteasomal degradation of oncogenic proteins including Akt, thereby inhibiting the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and enhancing apoptosis in ovarian cancer cells. Combination therapy of 17-AAG with cisplatin or taxol may improve therapeutic efficacy.

Article Title: Recent Advances in Ovarian Cancer: Therapeutic Strategies, Potential Biomarkers, and Technological Improvements
Actionable Recommendation: Target the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway using mTOR inhibitors (temsirolimus, ridaforolimus, everolimus) or dual PI3K/mTOR inhibitors (SPR965) to inhibit ovarian cancer cell growth and overcome chemoresistance. Akt inhibitors like afuresertib show promise in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer and are under clinical investigation.

Article Title: Recent Advances in Ovarian Cancer: Therapeutic Strategies, Potential Biomarkers, and Technological Improvements
Actionable Recommendation: Employ proteasome inhibitor PS341 (bortezomib) to prevent protein degradation, thereby inhibiting NF-κB activation and increasing chemotherapy-induced apoptosis in ovarian cancer treatment.

Article Title: Recent Advances in Ovarian Cancer: Therapeutic Strategies, Potential Biomarkers, and Technological Improvements
Actionable Recommendation: Utilize tubulin-targeting agents such as taxanes and vinca alkaloids, including novel formulations like polyglutamated paclitaxel (CT2103) and albumin-bound paclitaxel (Abraxane®), to improve efficacy and reduce side effects in ovarian cancer chemotherapy.

Article Title: Recent Advances in Ovarian Cancer: Therapeutic Strategies, Potential Biomarkers, and Technological Improvements
Actionable Recommendation: Develop and apply drug delivery systems (DDS), including nanoparticles and co-delivery systems, to enhance the delivery and efficacy of chemotherapeutic agents like cisplatin and paclitaxel, reduce toxicity, and overcome drug resistance in ovarian cancer treatment.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Use circulating CA125 levels combined with ascites leptin concentration as a predictive biomarker for prognosis and response to platinum-based chemotherapy in ovarian cancer patients.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Target CA125 with specific single-chain antibodies to downregulate its expression and increase cisplatin cytotoxicity, potentially overcoming platinum resistance in ovarian cancer cells.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Monitor serum HE4 levels during chemotherapy as a predictive biomarker for platinum resistance in ovarian cancer patients.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Consider mesothelin (MSLN) expression as a prognostic biomarker and potential therapeutic target in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer due to its association with worse progression-free and overall survival.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Apply hypomethylating agents such as azacitidine in combination with carboplatin to potentially restore platinum sensitivity in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer patients with DNA methylation alterations.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Use circulating miRNAs as predictive biomarkers for chemotherapy response and prognosis in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, although further validation is needed due to limited data and high cost.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Measure neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) and platelet-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) as simple, cost-effective prognostic and predictive biomarkers for platinum resistance and survival outcomes in ovarian cancer patients.

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer

Article Title: Role of Circulating Biomarkers in Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer

Combine CA125 and HE4 measurements to improve sensitivity and specificity for early detection of ovarian cancer recurrence and monitoring response to treatment.
Incorporate HE4 measurement in routine follow-up of epithelial ovarian cancer patients to detect recurrence earlier than CA125 alone, as HE4 can rise before CA125 in some cases.
Consider osteopontin (OPN) as an adjunct biomarker alongside CA125 for earlier detection of ovarian cancer recurrence, pending further clinical validation.

Use paraneoplastic antigen antibody panels (including HARS, Ro52, CDR2, 5H6) to predict ovarian cancer recurrence earlier than CA125 elevation or clinical relapse, especially when CA125 is below threshold.
Investigate microRNA (miRNA) panels as prognostic biomarkers for ovarian cancer recurrence and chemotherapy resistance, with potential for future clinical screening tools.
Target cancer stem cell (CSC) markers such as CD44, EpCAM, and ALDH1 for development of new therapeutic strategies and biomarkers to monitor ovarian cancer recurrence and chemoresistance.

Actionable Recommendation: Employ specific long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) such as H19, HOTAIR, XIST, UCA1, MALAT-1, PVT1, GAS5, HOXD-AS1, and SPRY4-IT1 as biomarkers for diagnosis, prognosis, and therapeutic targeting in ovarian cancer.

Target miRNAs therapeutically to modulate ovarian cancer progression and chemoresistance, including overexpression of tumor suppressor miRNAs or inhibition of oncogenic miRNAs.

Actionable Recommendation: Combine microRNA-204, CA125, and CA19.9 for improved early detection of ovarian tumors and cancer, as this combination shows the highest diagnostic performance with AUCs of 0.938, 1.000, and 0.998 for benign, early-stage, and late-stage ovarian cancers respectively.

Use serum IL-7 levels in combination with CA-125 measurements to improve the accuracy of distinguishing malignant ovarian tumors from benign pelvic masses, achieving 69% accurate classification without false positives for benign tumors.

Monitor serum IL-7 levels as a prognostic marker in ovarian cancer patients, as elevated IL-7 is associated with shorter overall survival and progression-free survival, although it is not an independent prognostic marker when accounting for clinical parameters.

Article Title: The Role of CA‐125 in the Management of Ovarian Cancer: A Systematic Review

Actionable Recommendation: For patients with elevated pre-treatment CA-125 levels (≥35 U/mL), classify them as high-risk for relapse or mortality and consider more aggressive monitoring and treatment strategies.

Article Title: Biological markers of prognosis, response to therapy and outcome in ovarian carcinoma
Actionable Recommendation: Use serum soluble folate receptor alpha (sFRA) levels as a diagnostic biomarker for ovarian carcinoma with greater accuracy than CA125, and as a prognostic marker associated with shorter progression-free survival.

Article Title: Biological markers of prognosis, response to therapy and outcome in ovarian carcinoma
Actionable Recommendation: Use methylation status of BRCA1 promoter as a biomarker for prognosis and platinum sensitivity in ovarian carcinoma patients.

Article Title: Biological markers of prognosis, response to therapy and outcome in ovarian carcinoma
Actionable Recommendation: Use BRCA1 and BRCA2 germline mutation status and homologous recombination repair deficiency as biomarkers to select ovarian carcinoma patients for PARP inhibitor therapy.

Article Title: Biological markers of prognosis, response to therapy and outcome in ovarian carcinoma
Actionable Recommendation: Consider BRCA mutation status and tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte frequency as biomarkers to select ovarian carcinoma patients likely to respond to PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint inhibitors.

Article Title: Biomarker-based early detection of epithelial ovarian cancer based on a five-protein signature in patient’s plasma – a prospective trial
Actionable Recommendation: Use a combined plasma protein panel including CA125, OPN, HE4, leptin, and prolactin to preoperatively differentiate between benign and malignant ovarian masses, improving diagnostic accuracy over single markers.

Article Title: Biomarkers and Strategies for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Use CA125 in combination with other biomarkers such as HE4 to improve sensitivity and specificity for early detection of ovarian cancer and to triage patients for specialized surgery.

Article Title: Biomarkers and Strategies for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Develop and use methylation-specific PCR panels targeting genes such as APC, RASSF1A, and OPCML for circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) detection to improve early-stage ovarian cancer diagnosis.

Article Title: Biomarkers and Strategies for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Apply the Risk of Ovarian Cancer Algorithm (ROCA) using serial CA125 measurements to individualize screening and improve early detection sensitivity.

Article Title: Biomarkers and algorithms for diagnosis of ovarian cancer: CA125, HE4, RMI and ROMA, a review
Actionable Recommendation: Use combined measurement of CA125 and HE4 serum levels to improve diagnostic accuracy for ovarian cancer, as this combination shows higher specificity and sensitivity than either marker alone.

Article Title: Biomarkers and algorithms for diagnosis of ovarian cancer: CA125, HE4, RMI and ROMA, a review
Actionable Recommendation: Interpret HE4 levels with caution in smokers and patients using estrogen plus progestin contraceptives, as these factors can alter HE4 serum levels and potentially lead to false positives or negatives.

Article Title: Biomarkers and algorithms for diagnosis of ovarian cancer: CA125, HE4, RMI and ROMA, a review
Actionable Recommendation: In cases of suspected benign ovarian tumors, measure both CA125 and HE4; an increase in both markers suggests malignancy and the need to consider surgical treatment.

Article Title: Biomarkers and algorithms for diagnosis of ovarian cancer: CA125, HE4, RMI and ROMA, a review
Actionable Recommendation: Use Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI) and Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA) incorporating CA125 and HE4 levels along with clinical parameters to stratify patients' risk and guide clinical decision-making.

Article Title: CBF-30228-ovarian-cancer-biomarkers-as-diagnostic-triage-tests
Actionable Recommendation: Use CA125 serum marker for monitoring recurrence of ovarian cancer and for diagnostic evaluation of known adnexal masses, but not for general population screening due to low sensitivity and specificity.

Article Title: CBF-30228-ovarian-cancer-biomarkers-as-diagnostic-triage-tests
Actionable Recommendation: Apply the Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI), which incorporates CA125, ultrasound findings, and menopausal status, as a cost-effective and reliable preoperative predictor to triage adnexal masses for referral to gynecologic oncologists.

Article Title: CBF-30228-ovarian-cancer-biomarkers-as-diagnostic-triage-tests
Actionable Recommendation: Use Human Epididymis Protein 4 (HE4) as a biomarker for monitoring recurrence or progression of epithelial ovarian cancer and as part of diagnostic algorithms, especially to improve detection in premenopausal women and early-stage disease.

Article Title: CBF-30228-ovarian-cancer-biomarkers-as-diagnostic-triage-tests
Actionable Recommendation: Employ the Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA), which combines CA125, HE4, and menopausal status, to improve preoperative risk stratification and referral decisions, particularly in postmenopausal women.

Article Title: CBF-30228-ovarian-cancer-biomarkers-as-diagnostic-triage-tests
Actionable Recommendation: Use OVA-1, a multi-biomarker algorithm including CA125 and additional markers, as a triage tool for women with adnexal masses to improve sensitivity of malignancy detection and appropriate referral to gynecologic oncologists, especially when clinical assessment is equivocal.
Investigate the PI3K/Akt and MAPK (ERK) pathways as downstream effectors of CD24-mediated EMT and tumor progression for potential combinational targeting.

Article Title: A comprehensive overview of exosomes in ovarian cancer: emerging biomarkers and therapeutic strategies
Actionable Recommendation: Use TUBB3 (tubulin beta-3 chain) as a potential biomarker for ovarian cancer diagnosis and prognosis.

Article Title: A comprehensive overview of exosomes in ovarian cancer: emerging biomarkers and therapeutic strategies
Actionable Recommendation: Monitor exosomal protein levels, including TGF-β1 and MAGE3/6, as biomarkers to distinguish ovarian cancer patients from those with benign tumors and to assess chemotherapy efficacy.

Article Title: A comprehensive overview of exosomes in ovarian cancer: emerging biomarkers and therapeutic strategies
Actionable Recommendation: Target annexin A3 to overcome platinum resistance in ovarian cancer by modulating exosome release and exocytosis.

Actionable Recommendation: Develop therapeutic strategies to inhibit exosomal ADAM15 ectodomain to block integrin-mediated MEK/ERK signaling pathway and inhibit ovarian cancer progression.

Actionable Recommendation: Utilize exosome-derived miRNAs such as miR-21, miR-103, miR-141, miR-203, miR-205, miR-214, miR-373, and miR-200a-c as prognostic biomarkers for ovarian cancer progression and poor prognosis.

Actionable Recommendation: Employ exosome-based delivery of miR-7 to inhibit ovarian cancer metastasis by downregulating EGFR/AKT/ERK1/2 signaling pathways.
Target CXCR4 to suppress epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) progression and enhance chemotherapy sensitivity, specifically in combination with paclitaxel (PTX) treatment.
Use CXCR4 expression levels as a biomarker to predict prognosis and chemotherapy resistance in EOC patients.
Employ PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway inhibitors (e.g., MK2206) to block CXCR4-mediated EMT, cancer stem cell stemness, invasion, and tumorigenesis in ovarian cancer cells.
Implement CXCR4 knockdown strategies to synergistically sensitize ovarian cancer cells to paclitaxel chemotherapy both in vitro and in vivo.

Article Title: Complementary Longitudinal Serum Biomarkers to CA125 for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Utilize HE4 and CA72-4 as complementary longitudinal serum biomarkers alongside CA125 to improve early detection sensitivity of ovarian cancer, especially for cases missed by CA125 alone.

Article Title: Complementary Longitudinal Serum Biomarkers to CA125 for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Exclude MMP-7 from future biomarker panels for early ovarian cancer detection due to minimal utility in identifying CA125-negative cases.

Article Title: Complementary Longitudinal Serum Biomarkers to CA125 for Early Detection of Ovarian Cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Implement multiplexed bead-based immunoassays for simultaneous measurement of CA125, HE4, MMP-7, and CA72-4 in ultra-low serum volumes to enable longitudinal biomarker monitoring.
Use HE4 and CA-125 as serum biomarkers for monitoring and detection of epithelial ovarian cancer, with HE4 showing slightly better performance than CA-125.

Develop and implement multivariate biomarker panels combining the nine most informative biomarkers (including HE4, CA-125, IL-2 receptor alpha, a1-antitrypsin, C-reactive protein, YKL-40, cellular fibronectin, CA-72-4, and prostasin) to improve sensitivity and specificity for ovarian cancer detection over existing panels such as OVA1.

Consider menopausal status when interpreting biomarker panel results, as biomarker performance (e.g., HE4, CA-125, IL-2 receptor alpha) is better in post-menopausal women compared to pre-menopausal women.

Use the CA-125 elimination rate constant K (KELIM) as a pragmatic biomarker to assess tumor chemosensitivity in advanced epithelial ovarian cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT). Calculate KELIM using at least 3 CA-125 measurements during the first 100 days of chemotherapy to predict likelihood of complete interval debulking surgery (IDS), progression-free survival (PFS), and overall survival (OS).

Integrate KELIM score with completeness of interval debulking surgery (IDS) to stratify patients into three prognostic groups (good, intermediate, poor) for personalized clinical decision-making and treatment planning.
Consider KELIM as a complementary tool to BRCA mutational status for predicting chemosensitivity and potentially guiding the use of PARP inhibitors, especially in patients with BRCA2 mutations.

For patients with unfavorable KELIM scores and incomplete IDS (poor prognosis group), consider innovative treatment approaches to increase chemosensitivity, such as chemotherapy dosage adjustment (fractionated dose-dense chemotherapy), addition of bevacizumab, or cell cycle checkpoint inhibitors.

Article Title: Detection of the Ovarian Cancer Biomarker Lysophosphatidic Acid in Serum
Actionable Recommendation: Develop and implement a biosensor assay based on the actin–gelsolin protein complex to detect lysophosphatidic acid (LPA) in serum for early-stage ovarian cancer screening, with a detection limit of approximately 5 µM LPA.

Article Title: Detection of the Ovarian Cancer Biomarker Lysophosphatidic Acid in Serum
Actionable Recommendation: Use gelsolin domains 1–3 (gelsolin1–3) as a probe protein in the biosensor assay to improve surface binding and signal strength compared to full-length gelsolin.

Article Title: Detection of the Ovarian Cancer Biomarker Lysophosphatidic Acid in Serum
Actionable Recommendation: Optimize the biosensor surface preparation by using silica gel with a pore size of 150 Å, a 1:2 ratio of MEG-Cl linker to spacer, and a plasma cleaning time of at least 10 minutes to reduce non-specific adsorption and improve assay reproducibility.

Article Title: Detection of the Ovarian Cancer Biomarker Lysophosphatidic Acid in Serum
Actionable Recommendation: Employ fluorescence spectroscopy measurement of rhodamine-dyed actin release from the actin–gelsolin complex as a quantitative readout for LPA concentration in serum samples.

Use serum human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) as a diagnostic biomarker to differentiate ovarian cancer from benign gynecologic diseases due to its higher sensitivity and better overall diagnostic accuracy compared to soluble mesothelin-related protein (SMRP).
Combine HE4 with CA-125 serum tumor markers to improve sensitivity and specificity for ovarian cancer diagnosis compared to using either marker alone.
Consider combining HE4 and SMRP markers to further increase diagnostic accuracy for ovarian cancer, as this combination showed the highest diagnostic odds ratio among tested combinations.
Use serum SMRP with caution as a diagnostic marker for ovarian cancer due to its limited sensitivity despite high specificity; it may be more suitable for confirming diagnosis rather than screening.

Use urine HE4 concentration and HE4/creatinine (HE4/CRE) ratio as non-invasive tumor markers for differential diagnosis of ovarian cancer, distinguishing cancer from healthy individuals or benign conditions.
Implement urine HE4/CRE ratio measurement for early detection of chemotherapy resistance in ovarian cancer patients to guide therapy adjustments.
Use serial measurements of urine HE4/CRE ratio in follow-up of ovarian cancer patients to detect disease recurrence earlier than serum markers or clinical signs.
Standardize preanalytical procedures for urine collection, processing, and HE4 measurement, including reporting HE4 normalized to creatinine, to improve diagnostic accuracy and reproducibility.
Consider urine HE4 testing as a non-invasive alternative to serum HE4 for ovarian cancer diagnosis and monitoring, especially in early-stage disease and in patients where blood sampling is difficult.

Article Title: Discovery of a biomarker candidate for surgical stratification in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
Actionable Recommendation: Investigate therapeutic targeting of oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) pathways in ALG5-high HGSOC tumors, as ALG5 expression correlates strongly with OXPHOS gene signatures, suggesting a distinct metabolic phenotype that may be susceptible to OXPHOS inhibitors.

Use a panel combining CA-125 and HE4 biomarkers, calculated via the Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm (ROMA), to improve differentiation between benign and malignant ovarian tumors, especially in preoperative assessment of adnexal masses.

Adjust cut-off values for CA-125, HE4, and RMI to improve specificity in ovarian cancer diagnosis: increase CA-125 cut-off to ≥60 U/mL, RMI to ≥250, and HE4 to 140 pmol/L in postmenopausal women to reduce false positives while maintaining acceptable sensitivity.

Prefer HE4 and ROMA markers over CA-125 and RMI for distinguishing benign ovarian tumors or endometriosis from ovarian cancer, particularly in premenopausal women, due to their higher specificity and lower false positive rates in benign gynecological conditions.

In clinical settings where pelvic ultrasonography is performed by less experienced sonographers, incorporate HE4 measurement to improve diagnostic accuracy and triage of women presenting with adnexal lesions.

Actionable Recommendation: Use the ATC chip to isolate and molecularly profile ascites tumor cells (ATCs) from ovarian cancer patients to monitor treatment response and tumor progression through serial sampling of ascites fluid.

Actionable Recommendation: Apply the quad-marker panel (MUC-1, EGFR, HER2, EpCAM) with μNMR technology for rapid, sensitive, and multiplexed detection of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in ovarian cancer patients to improve diagnosis and monitor disease progression.

Actionable Recommendation: Employ the nPLEX nano-plasmonic exosome detection platform to sensitively and rapidly detect and molecularly profile ovarian cancer-derived exosomes in patient ascites and blood samples, using markers such as EpCAM and CD24, to diagnose cancer and monitor treatment response.

Monitor metabolic pathways related to fatty acid β-oxidation, phospholipid metabolism, and bile acid metabolism in ovarian cancer patients to understand disease progression and therapeutic response.

Explore inhibition of diacylglycerol kinase (DGK) α to promote T cell activation and enhance cancer immunotherapy efficacy in ovarian cancer.

Article Title: Predicting chemoresponsiveness in epithelial ovarian cancer patients using circulating small extracellular vesicle-derived plasma gelsolin
Actionable Recommendation: Use the ratio of total plasma gelsolin (pGSN) to small extracellular vesicle-derived plasma gelsolin (sEV-pGSN) measured from patient plasma samples prior to chemotherapy as a predictive biomarker to identify epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) patients likely to be chemoresistant (progression free interval ≤ 12 months). This biomarker ratio outperforms CA125 and individual pGSN measurements in predicting chemoresponsiveness and can inform treatment planning and follow-up strategies.

Monitor serum CA125 levels dynamically, specifically calculating the CA125 half-life, to predict progression-free survival (PFS) in patients with epithelial ovarian carcinoma (EOC). Use CA125 half-life as an independent prognostic biomarker to identify patients at higher risk of disease progression and adjust individualized treatment plans accordingly.

Use TGF-β2 mRNA and protein expression levels as predictive biomarkers for poor prognosis in ovarian cancer patients, especially those with serous, poorly differentiated, advanced-stage tumors, TP53 mutations, or undergoing chemotherapy with platins, Taxol, or a combination of platin+Taxol.

Develop and investigate inhibitors targeting TGF-β2 to potentially improve clinical outcomes in ovarian cancer patients, especially those with poor prognosis markers.

Use Nrf2 expression as an independent prognostic biomarker to identify ovarian carcinoma patients with poorer disease-free survival (DFS) and overall survival (OS), particularly in serous carcinoma subtype.
Consider targeting Nrf2 for therapeutic intervention to overcome platinum-based chemotherapy resistance in ovarian epithelial carcinoma, especially in serous carcinoma with high Nrf2 expression.

Use p16 immunoexpression as a prognostic marker in univariate analysis to predict disease-free survival and overall survival in ovarian carcinoma, with caution as it loses significance in multivariate analysis.
Monitor Keap1 expression levels in ovarian carcinoma subtypes, especially serous carcinoma, as it shows highest cytoplasmic immunoexpression, although it was not an independent prognostic factor.

Evaluate E-cadherin expression as a biomarker for tumor subtype differentiation and potential involvement in tumor proliferation and metastasis, though it was not a significant prognostic factor in this study.

Use combined immunohistochemical analysis of p62, LC3, and Beclin1 expression as prognostic and predictive biomarkers in epithelial ovarian carcinoma (EOC) to guide clinical management and therapy decisions.

Incorporate autophagy marker expression profiling (p62, LC3, Beclin1) in histopathological evaluation of ovarian tumors to assess tumor aggressiveness, stage, and differentiation, aiding in risk stratification.

Consider combining autophagy inhibitors with platinum-based chemotherapy in aggressive epithelial ovarian carcinoma subtypes exhibiting high LC3 and p62 expression to potentially overcome chemoresistance.
